figures.gloss: The Great White God — the bright supreme sky-lord of the Mari, head of a household of subordinate jumo of sun, wind, birth and grain, prayed to in the sacred groves for bread, herds and peace, his offerings white beasts and white garments.
figures.facet: The sky-father joint still under active worship: where his Finnic kin survive only in reconstructed epic, Osh Kugu Jumo still receives grove sacrifice in Mari El — the one sky-father of pagan Europe with an unbroken congregation, the shard's whole reason for being.
figures.provenance: Uno Harva (Holmberg), Die Religion der Tscheremissen (1926); Sebeok & Ingemann, Studies in Cheremis: The Supernatural (1956); post-Soviet practice documented by Sonja Luehrmann and Mari El fieldwork.

figures.gloss: The dark counterpart and his fenced enclosure in one word: the keremet spirit — in some tellings Jumo's brother who spoiled the creation — receives black animals in a walled grove set apart from the white god's kusoto, feared, fed and never neglected.
figures.facet: The sacred split into a two-sided offering economy rather than a war: the dark power is not exorcised but kept on the books, paid in black beasts at his own fenced address — adversary and genius loci fused into a working institution.
figures.provenance: Harva 1926 and 19th-c. Kazan-diocese ethnography; the keremet institution and its name (ultimately Arabic karāma via Volga Bulgar Islam) are shared across the Volga-Kama peoples.

figures.gloss: The küsoto, the consecrated sacred grove where the kart priests in white lead communal prayer and animal sacrifice with porridge, pancakes and altar-fires — several hundred groves survive in Mari El, some under legal protection, and the great collective prayers still fill them.
figures.facet: Europe's only living pagan sacrificial liturgy: not a revival and not a reconstruction but a continuity — the grove as a functioning temple whose priesthood (the karts) never lapsed, which is precisely the asymmetry this shard holds against its reconstructed Finnic kin.
figures.provenance: 16th-c. Muscovite reports onward; Sebeok & Ingemann 1956 on kart and grove; contemporary documentation of Mari El grove prayers (mer kumaltysh) in post-Soviet ethnography and journalism on 'Europe's last pagans'.

figures.gloss: The Earth Mother, foremost of the Mari ava — the 'mothers' of earth, water, sun, wind and fire (Mlande Ava, Vüd Ava, Keche Ava) who personify each element as a generative female keeper to be asked before it is used.
figures.facet: Divinity organised as a system of mothers: the female generative principle is not one great goddess but distributed across the whole working landscape — every element the farmer touches has an ava to be addressed, fertility as an administration.
figures.provenance: The ava-series is catalogued in Harva 1926 and in Sebeok & Ingemann's Cheremis deity lists (1956).

figures.gloss: The two registers of Mari religiosity: the chi marij ('pure Mari'), never baptized, keeping the grove faith whole; and the marla vera, baptized Mari who hold Orthodoxy and grove sacrifice together in one life — icon corner in the house, kusoto beyond the village.
figures.facet: Survival by held tension rather than isolation: the tradition endured five centuries of Christian pressure not by hiding but by institutionalising its own dual membership — the unbroken shard kept unbroken partly by those who also carried the other faith.
figures.provenance: The chi marij / marla vera distinction is standard in Mari studies (Harva 1926; post-Soviet work by Luehrmann and Mari scholars on 'Mari dvoeverie').

edges.note: Mari jumo and Finnish jumala share the Finno-Volgaic god-word *juma — a real lexical cognate; the offices grew apart, and the deeper seam is the asymmetry: Ukko is reconstructed from song while Jumo still eats grove sacrifice.

The holy forest as the place where the god is met — but the kusoto is a consecrated sanctuary with priest and altar-fire while Tapio's wood is the wild itself; kin peoples, different offices of the forest.

Mother-powers of Finnic kin peoples, met at different scales: Ilmatar is the one oceanic mother of the cosmogony, Mlande Ava one of a working system of element-mothers tending the tilled land — the same joint, cosmic versus agrarian register.
White god of the open grove against the dark keeper of the fenced enclosure — offerings split between them by colour and kind; a maintained polarity of the sacred, not a war of good and evil.

One Volga keremet institution shared across Chuvash, Mari and Udmurt villages — the word itself (Arabic karāma via Bulgar Islam) travelled with the fenced-grove cult; a documented areal kinship, though the exact direction of borrowing is debated.

Neighbouring supreme gods of one Volga-Kama religious landscape — a Turkic name and a Uralic name filling the same office of grove-worshipped high sky under the same Orthodox pressure; areal kinship, not descent.

Two rooms of the one Volga-Kama sacrificial complex — communal animal-and-porridge offering at a fixed holy place; the kinship of the institutions is plain, but specific descent between them is untraced, so tiered as analogy.

Neighbouring Volga-Kama supreme sky-gods with parallel histories — each grove-worshipped, each partly fused with the Christian God under the same dual-faith pressure; areal kinship of office, no descent claimed between the cults.

Two rooms of one Volga sanctuary landscape — the Udmurt family log-shrine (whose name answers the Mari kudo, both from Uralic *kota) beside the Mari communal grove; kindred institutions at different social scales.

The great communal grove sacrifice of the Volga-Kama complex in its Udmurt and Mari forms — plainly one regional family of practice, but specific descent between the two rites is untraced, so tiered as analogy.

dossier.essence: The ethnic religion of the Mari (Cheremis), a Volga-Finnic people of the middle Volga, centered in the Mari El republic of Russia. Its central claim: the world is kept in order under the bright sky-god Osh Kugu Jumo and a household of subordinate gods and element-mothers, whose favor is held by communal prayer and animal sacrifice in consecrated groves. It is the one indigenous paganism of Europe practiced in continuity rather than revival, which is why journalists call the Mari 'Europe's last pagans.'
dossier.cosmos: The Great White God presides over a bright upper world, while the working landscape below is distributed among ava, 'mothers' — Mlande Ava of the earth, Vüd Ava of the water, Keche Ava of the sun — each a generative female keeper to be addressed before her element is used. Against the white side stands the keremet: a dark, exacting spirit-class paid in black animals at its own fenced enclosure, feared and fed but never at war with heaven — the sacred kept as a two-sided ledger. The dead join the ancestors and remain parties to village life, remembered and fed at fixed memorial days.
dossier.practice: Worship is communal prayer and sacrifice (kumaltysh) in the küsoto, the consecrated grove, led by kart priests in white: animal offerings with ritual porridge and pancakes cooked at altar-fires, scaled from village rites up to great collective prayers. Several hundred groves survive in Mari El, some under legal protection, and the large public prayers resumed openly after 1991. Mari religiosity runs in two registers — the chi marij ('pure Mari'), never baptized, and the marla vera, baptized Mari who hold Orthodoxy and grove sacrifice together in one life.
dossier.canon: No scripture: the canon is an oral corpus of prayers, charms, and grove liturgy, put on paper by ethnographers — Harva's 'Die Religion der Tscheremissen' (1926) and Sebeok and Ingemann's 'Studies in Cheremis: The Supernatural' (1956) are the standard written records.
dossier.reflects: Continuity itself as the sacred achievement — the impulse to keep the covenant with a local landscape unbroken, absorbing five centuries of imperial and church pressure by institutionalizing dual membership rather than hiding.
sources: Uno Holmberg, 'Finno-Ugric, Siberian (The Mythology of All Races, Vol. IV)' (1927)
Thomas A. Sebeok and Frances J. Ingemann, 'Studies in Cheremis: The Supernatural' (1956)
Sonja Luehrmann, 'Secularism Soviet Style: Teaching Atheism and Religion in a Volga Republic' (2011)
